This progress you will see easily in that old English ballad Turpin Hero which begins in the first person and ends in the third person. The dramatic form is reached when the vitality which has flowed and eddied around each person fills every person with such vital force that he or she assumes a proper and intangible esthetic life. The personality ofthe artist, at first a cry or a cadence or a mood and then a fluid and lambent narrative, finally refines itself out of existence, impersonalises itself, so to speak. The esthetic image in the dramatic form is life purified in and reprojected from the human imagination. The mystery of esthetic like that of material creation is accomplished. The artist, like the God of the creation, remains within or behind or beyond or above his handiwork, invisible, refined out of existence, indifferent, paring his fingernails. Joyce, James. from Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man . p. 229-33.

title: Dante. Purgatory, Canto I.
text: The Divine Comedy "Purgatory" (1321), Canto II Dante Alighieri The poet comes to Purgatory, beholds four stars seen only by our first parents, and meets Cato of Utica. O'ER better waves to speed her rapid course The light bark of my genius lifts the sail, Well pleased to leave so cruel sea behind; And of that second region will I sing, In which the human spirit from sinful blot Is purged, and for ascent to Heaven prepares. Here, O ye hallow'd Nine! for in your train I follow, here the deaden'd strain revive; Nor let Calliope refuse to sound A somewhat higher song, of that loud tone Which when the wretched birds of chattering note Had heard, they of forgiveness lost all hope. Sweet hue of eastern sapphire, that was spread O'er the serene aspect of the pure air, High up as the first circle, to mine eyes Unwonted joy renew'd, soon as I 'scaped Forth from the atmosphere of deadly gloom, That had mine eyes and bosom fill'd with grief. The radiant planet, that to love invites, Made all the orient laugh, and veil'd beneath The Pisces' light, that in his escort came. - To the right hand I turn'd, and fix'd my mind On the other pole attentive, where I saw Four stars ne'er seen before save by the ken Of our first parents. Heaven of their rays Seem'd joyous. O thou northern site! bereft Indeed, and widow'd, since of these deprived. As from this view I had desisted, straight Turning a little toward the other pole, There from whence now the wain had disappear'd, I saw an old man standing by my side Alone, so worthy of reverence in his look, That ne'er from son to father more was owed. Low down his beard, and mix'd with hoary white, Descended, like his locks, which, parting, fell Upon his breast in double fold. The beams Of those four luminaries on his face So brightly shone, and with such radiance clear Deck'd it, that I beheld him as the sun. "Say who are ye, that stemming the blind stream, Forth from the eternal prison-house have fled?" He spoke and moved those venerable plumes. "Who hath conducted, or with lantern sure Lights you emerging from the depth of night, That makes the infernal valley ever black? Are the firm statutes of the dread abyss Broken, or in high heaven new laws ordain'd, That thus, condemn'd, ye to my caves approach?" My guide, then laying hold on me, by words And intimations given with hand and head, Made my bent knees and eye submissive pay Due reverence; then thus to him replied: "Not of myself I come; a Dame from heaven Descending, him besought me in my charge To bring. But since thy will implies, that more Our true condition I unfold at large, Mine is not to deny thee thy request. This mortal ne'er hath seen the furthest gloom; But erring by his folly had approach'd So near, that little space was left to turn. Then, as before I told, I was despatch'd To work his rescue; and no way remain'd Save this which I have ta'en. I have display'd Before him all the regions of the bad; And purpose now those spirits to display, That under thy command are purged from sin. How I have brought him would be long to say. From high descends the virtue, by whose aid I to thy sight and hearing him have led. Now may our coming please thee. In the search Of liberty he journeys: that how dear, They know who for her sake have life refused. Thou knowest, to whom death for her was sweet In Utica, where thou didst leave those weeds, That in the last great day will shine so bright. He breathes, and I of Minos am not bound, For us the eternal edicts are unmoved Abiding in that circle, where the eyes Of thy chaste Marcia beam, who still in look Prays thee, O hallow'd spirit! to own her thine. Then by her love we implore thee, let us pass Through thy seven regions; for which, best thanks I for thy favor will to her return, If mention there below thou not disdain." "Marcia so pleasing in my sight was found," He then to him rejoin'd, "while I was there, That all she ask'd me I was fain to grant. Now that beyond the accursed stream she dwells, She may no longer move me, by that law, Which was ordain'd me, when I issued thence. Not so, if Dame from heaven, as thou sayst, Moves and directs thee; then no flattery needs. Enough for me that in her name thou ask. Go therefore now: and with a slender reed See that thou duly gird him, and his face Lave, till all sordid stain thou wipe from thence. For not with eye, by any cloud obscured, Would it be seemly before him to come, Who stands the foremost minister in Heaven. This islet all around, there far beneath, Where the wave beats it, on the oozy bed Produces store of reeds. No other plant, Cover'd with leaves, or harden'd in its stalk, There lives, not bending to the water After, this way return not; but the sun Will show you, that now rises, where to take The mountain in its easiest ascent." He disappear'd; and I myself upraised Speechless, and to my guide retiring close, Toward him turn'd mine eyes. He thus began: "My son! observant thou my steps pursue. We must retreat to rereward; for that way The champain to its low extreme declines." The dawn had chased the matin hour of prime, Which fled before it, so that from afar I spied the trembling of the ocean stream. We traversed the deserted plain, as one Who, wander'd from his track, thinks every step Trodden in vain till he regain the path. When we had come, where yet the tender dew Strove with the sun, and in a place where fresh The wind breathed o'er it, while it slowly dried; Both hands extended on the watery grass My master placed, in graceful act and kind. Whence I of his intent before apprised, Stretch'd out to him my cheeks suffused with tears. There to my visage he anew restored That hue which the dun shades of hell conceal'd. Then on the solitary shore arrived, That never sailing on its waters saw Man that could after measure back his course, He girt me in such manner as had pleased Him who instructed; and, oh strange to tell! As he selected every humble plant, Wherever one was pluck'd another there Resembling, straightway in its place arose. Dante Alighieri. The Divine Comedy . "Purgatory", Canto I. Transl. Henry F. Carey.

title: Chicago
text: Semite Bleistein is identified as a Semite. Since Jews were always so independent of nation, having none of their own, they have been recognisable throughout much of the history of time and were never afraid to settle in new countries, often without losing their own identity and tradition. Thus Bleistein, as a Semite, has a better sense of the past than Burbank and remembers Venice's uglier, less Romantic sides. reference topics Burbank and anti-Semitism The City and the Stranger Bleistein and Misrepresentation Burbank and Bleistein back to "Burbank"

title: The Serious Poet
text: The Serious Artist '… Mais d'abord il faut etre un poete', as MM Duhamel and Vildrae have said. - Ezra Pound. ( Literary Essays of Ezra Pound , p. 9.) Introduction The reappearance of the lost manuscript of The Waste Land caused amazement, wonder, and, in some cases indignation. What had previously emerged from letters now become unmistably clear: Pound's influence on the long poem had been significant - lines were scrapped, passages removed or kept in, epigraphs changed and so forth. Some critics have deplored Pound's severe cutting, being of the opinion that a great poem could have been even greater. Other critics say that the poem would just have been bigger and point out that Eliot was free to take Pound's advice or leave it. Some of the excised sections have grown into complete and independent poems, such as "The Hollow Men" which has developed from the section called Dream Songs ; other fragments published more or less as they were when Pound suggested them out of The Waste Land . For some, this is evidence that Eliot regretted the loss of the passages, or did not in fact agree with Pound but submitted to his authority nevertheless. There are other critics who will point out that by doing so, Pound strengthened the poem considerably; Hugh Kenner, in The Invisible Poet , implies that by cutting out all passages that did not attain the highest form of intensity, Pound improved the overal quality of the poem, and furthermore discovered a unity in the long fragmentary poem which Eliot himself did not recognise at first, having been lost in creating a complicated and associative stream of consciousness that built on Browning's experimentation with the dramatic monologue and the concept of finiteness. Critics of this idea, however, will say that the unity was never meant to be there. There are even critics who do not see a unity in the poem in its present form. One important side effect of this extensive discusion, however, is that more attention has since been paid to Pound's influence on Eliot as a friend and critic: Pound's successful introduction of both vers libre in general and T.S. Eliot poetry in particular to the English and American public. Then more first drafts appeared with corrections, comments, and suggestions in Pound's hand. Memories of 'literary sessions' start popping up here and there among the criticism - comments from interviews, anecdotes, memories, about how and when the idea to use the quatrain form arose. Wyndham Lewis remembers that although certainly there are certianly links with Prufrock, technically, "Gerontion" is "school of Ezra". Southam, who's greatest offense is producing an excellent guide without a clear attribution of sources, attributes the inspiration for a whole poem to Pound: the "Burbank" poem. Slowly but steadily, a more complete picture of the literary friendship between the two men stumbles into the critical daylight, undoubtedly having taken so long partly because of Pound's fascist faux pas in the second World War. One large area that thus far remains unexplored, however, is the technical one. The interest for the technical aspect of poetry may be waning in contemporary criticism, but we might nevertheless wonder what Wyndham Lewis meant precisely when he said that Gerontion was "school of Ezra"? In the rest of this section, I will try to make an overview of Pound's most prominent and important ideas - in short, to provide a little pamphlet which recreates this "school of Ezra", which behests more than imagism or vorticism alone. While doing so, I have focussed my research primarily on the Literary Essays from Ezra Pound (F&F, 1954), which T.S. Eliot has compiled and edited and to which he wrote an introduction. The collection is very suitable for the task at hand: the editor ... wished to regard the material in historical perspective, to put a new generation of readers, into whose hands the earlier collections and scattered essays did not come when they were new, into a position to appreciate the central importance of Pound's critical writing in the development of poetry during the first half of the twentieth century. ... I hope, furthermore, that this volume will demonstrate that Pound's literary criticism is the most important criticism of its kind. Of a very important kind - perhaps the kind that we an least afford to do without… ( Literary Essays , p. x) Eliot himself alots Pound's critical writing a role of "central importance" in the development of modernist poetry; already not uninteresting in itself, for any scholar who wishes to ascertain Pound's influence on Eliot, the latter poet's own perspective on Pound's historical importance in the field of literature is extremely interesting; after all, can it ever be wholly impartial? It will be Eliot's appreciation of Pound's importance, not anyone else's and therefore we will with great interest read what Eliot has to say about why Pound's crticism is 'vital': That Pound has said much about the art of writing and of writing poetry in particular, that is perminantly valid and useful. Very few critics have done that. That he said much that was peculiarly pertinent to the needs of the time at which it was written. That he forced upon our attention not only individual authors, but whole areas of poetry which no further criticism can afford to ignore. (ibid., p. ) Fourty-five years later, we can ourselves ask a few questions, such as whether what Pound wrote in the first half of this century is still valid and useful today. Undoubtedly many a critic will have done without these areas of poetry and not think he or she is any worse for having ignored Pound. The millions of poets that the English language is rich today will think they have done perfectly well without Ezra Pound and his ideas. Nevertheless, Eliot's second point is very important to any investigation not only of modernist poetry in general, but particularly of Eliot's own poetry. Due to the fragmentary nature of Pound's writings, an aspect of his critical work which Pound himself recognised, I have made an attempt to create a more coherent whole. If by doing so I have reconstructed a unity that was never there, I hope at least to have made a convincing argument of the kind of unity that Eliot could see in Pound, just like Pound saw in The Waste Land . I. The Serious Artist - the Artist as Scientist The subject which the artists study is man. This subject he should treat with respect and with care. Does he follow the method of the scientist, his artistic 'reports' will be true, reliable, and lasting. He will have used sufficient amounts of data, for instance like the biologist who carefully observes "over 100 cultures from the secretions of the respiratory tracts of over 500 patients and 30 nurses and attendants." Then, he treats this data with respect: "The results of each observation must be precise and no single observation must in itself be taken as determining a general law, although after experiment, certain observations may be held as typical or normal." ( Literary Essays , p. 46) As a result, he will produce good art. If he follows the method of the theorist, basing his report on single observations or on his own wishes and desires, he will produce bad art. Bad art is immoral and should be punished, for: If a scientist falsifies a report either deliberately or through negligence we consider him as either a criminal or a bad scientist according to the enormity of his offence, and he is punished or despised accordingly. ( Literary Essays , p. 43) Therefore, an artist will base his report on a number of careful observations, making perhaps only a few discoveries in his life, in any. The life of the serious artist is not easy. Whereas a serious scientist such as Marconi is thrilled with the discovery of, for instance, radiowaves, and so are his fellow scientists, "the public so dear to the journalistic heart, is far more concerned with the shareholders in the Marconi company." The problem for the artist is possibly even bigger, for his subject of study is at the same time often his audience. If his diagnosis of the aegrum vulgus is that his audience cannot read, he will have a hard time making himself understood in writing and may have to devise a sign-language. If he cannot, fortunatetly, the results of the serious artist are durable: The permanent property, the property given to the race at large is precisely these data of the serious scientist and of the serious artist; of the scientist as touching the relations of abstract numbers of molecular energy, of the composition of matter, etc.; of the serious artist, as touching the nature of man, of individuals. (ibid., p. 47) These results, carefully recorded in his art, can hence be of avail to another scientist. "As there are in medicine the art of diagnosis and the art of cure, so in the arts, so in the particular arts of poetry and of literature, there is the art of diagnosis and there is the art of cure." (ibid., p. 46) It is therefore that the serious artist shall study with care the good work that has stood the test of time, re-evaluating and learning the processes, methods, observations and suggested cures of the past. After all, "the scientist does not expect to be acclaimed as a great scientist until he has discovered something. He begins by learning what has been discovered already. He goes from that point onwards." A small discussion of two of Pound's examples of such anthropoligical observations will further elucidate matters: If one finds it convenient to think in chronological cycles, and wants to 'relate literature to history', I suggest the three convenient 'breaks' or collapses. The fall of Alexander's Macedonian empire; the fall of the Roman empire; the collapse of Italy after 1500, the fall of Lodivico Moro, and the sack of Rome. That is to say, human lucidity appears to have approached several times a sort of maximum, and then suffered a set-back. (ibid., p. 40) Here we see the serious artist at work. He makes observations, about literature and history; he gathers his evidence, makes generalisations, and presents a diagnosis. Of course, a great number of observations that went into this investigation have not made it into this report or were left implicit. It is now our task to verify the observation independently. If we accept the diagnosis, we can proceed to look for a cure, should there be any. Perhaps we can study the three respective breaks in more detail and attempt to learn the cause of this set-back, so that we may prevent or delay such a set-back in our own future. The second observation is more specific, and clearer: consider the Victory of Samothrace and the Taj of Agra. The man who carved the one and the man who designed the other may either or both of them have looked like an ape, or like two apes respectively. They may have looked like other apelike or swinelike men. We have the Victory and the Taj to witness that there was something within them differing from the contents of apes and of other swinelike men. Thus we learn that humanity is a species or genus of animals capable of a variation that will produce the desire for a Taj or a Victory, and moreover capable of effecting that Taj or Victory in stone. We know from other testimony of the arts and from ourselves that the desire often overshoots the power of efficient presentation; we therefore conclude that other members of the race may have desired to effect a Taj or a Victory. We even suppose that men have desired to effect more beautiful things although few of us are capable of forming any precise mental image of things, in their particular way, more beautiful than this statue or this building. So difficult is this that no one has yet been able to effect a restoration for the missing head of the Victory. At least no one has done so in stone, so far as I know. Doubtless many people have stood opposite the statue and made such heads in their imagination. (ibid., p. 44) Again this is a diagnosis. We have a statue and a building of great beauty, effected by men who do not look like educated white Englishmen. One of them probably looked like an Indian, or perhaps a Pers or a Turk. The other possibly like an ancient Greek, or an African. Nevertheless, in both of these men of different races was the desire for the creation of a beautiful object, and moreover the capability of effecting this beautiful object in stone. If we look at the example more closely, however, we can also see a combination of diagnosis and cure at work behind this diagnosis. Behind the references to ape and swinelike men there is unmistakably Pound's observation of the folly of many of his contempories over-enthusiastic embracement of Darwin's theory of evolution as a means to deprecate other races. His cure is the observation of the beauty that these so called swinelike and apelike people can produce. The art of cure is also called ethics and practiced by several people, including a number of them who are not fitted for the task at hand, because their methods are unsound. An example of such a criminal is the social theoretician, an anthropological disease of which the English writers about sex are the most prominant symptoms: "The theorist, and we see this constantly illustrated by the English writers on sex, the theorist constantly proceeds as if his own case, his own limits and predilictions were the typical case, or even as if it were the universal. He is constantly urging someone else to behave as he, the theorist, would like to behave." (ibid., p. 46) The serious artist will do no such thing. The serious artist will collect his data and concern himself with producing an efficient representation of this data. This data may be anything - it may be the artists own desire, or hate, or indifference, towards a certain observation, idea, concept or image - but the serious artist will always present it as such: an object of his own desire or hate. He will not force his own behaviour on that of someone else, like the false theorist would do. A serious artist, like a serious scientist, will present his diagnosis, or his cure, or both, and the reader will be free to take or leave this diagnosis or cure. As such, the best data for anthropological studies, for ethics, for the teaching of morality, for the determination of social norms, is good art. For good art "bears witness of and define for us the inner nature and conditions of man." (ibid., p. 4). And a final warning In the writers of the duo-cento and early tre-cento we find a precise psychology, embedded in a now almost unintelligible jargon, but there nevertheless. If we cannot get back to these things; if the serious artist cannot attain this precision in verse, then he must either take to prose or give up his claim to being a serious artist. [!] (54) Or, at leat, the artist may demonstrate that he has made a conscientious effort to reach this goal, and if he failed then by adhering to the strictures that a serious artist should oppose to himself, he will have paved the way for a more successful attempt by one of his successors. In short, the serious artist mirrors himself to the serious scientist, in method of observation and research. He will study the work of his ancestors with great diligence; he will make his observations with accuracy and care. II The Problem of Presentation - The Serious Artist's Implements So, the young but serious artist commences his long journey with his eye opened. Left and right he makes observations. Perhaps he notices that while there is poverty and children die of hunger, the Bishop of London's encourages "this sort of increase" among his poor subjects. He might observe that as such the desires of the Bishop for more influence are projected onto his ethics rather than that he bases his government on careful scientific observation. He might contrast this with the shady activities of the souteneur, and wonder which one of the two is the more detestable (c.f. ibid ., p. 42) After making a few more such observations and reading up on the literature so far produced by other serious artists and scientists, the serious artist wishes to present his results in the proper manner. What to do? First of all, the serious artist must divorce himself (again) from the concerns of the public opinion. The public "will not consider the 'problem of style'. They want 'The value of art to life' and 'Fundamental issues'" Nevertheless, for the serious artist style is immensely important. He wishes to communicate his observations to his readers, current and future, and to his fellow artists. He desires to present his image with the maximum possible clarity, but stumbles across the boundaries of his material: language . Fortunately for him, the serious artist is not alone: Man desires to communicate with his fellows. He desires an ever increasing complicated communication. Gestures serves up to a point. Symbols may serve. When you desire something not present to the eye or when you desire to communicate ideas, you must have recourse to speech. Gradually you wish to communicate something less bare and ambiguous than ideas. You wish to communicate an idea and its modifications, an idea and a crowd of its effects, atmospheres, contradictions. You wish to question whether a certain formula works in every case, or in what per cent of cases, etc. etc. etc. you get the Henry James novel. (ibid., p. 51) All the litterati have the same problem, and will have tried to deal with this problem in increasingly advanced manners. They will occasionally make a new invention, a new way of saying something, and just like the discoveries of a physicist these inventions are usually of lasting usefulness. Again the study of other sciences will aid the serious artist in taking stock of his precedents: We apply a loose-leaf system to book-keeping so as to have the live items separated from the dead ones. In the study of physics we begin with simple mechanisms, wedge, lever and fulcrum, pully and inclined plane, all of them still as useful as when they were first invented. We proceed by a study of inventions. We are not asked to memorize a lot of the parts of a side-wheeler engine. ( ibid. , p. 18-9) Similarly, throughout the history of poetry, there are "simple discoveries, clearly marked", discovered by a few men of genius in each historical age of literature. The bulk of the rest of the works consists of followers who further experiment with the fine details of the new principle, who wish simply to understand the new invention, or find it as an indispensible tool for presenting their own images or observations in other fields. Pound has proposed, several times, to produce a work that contains a historical overview of these inventions, but the usual problems presented themselves and he could not overcome them; most notably, there were fears with publishers that such a revaluation of the past would also devaluate the "thousands of pounds sterling in electro-plate" ( ibid ., p.18). The serious artist will do well to mark his words, that "against ignorance one might struggle, and even against organic stupidity [universities, TLS ], but against so vast a vested interest the lone odds were too heavy." Pound departed for the banks of the Seine, later followed by Ulysses , where he proceeded his study of inventions, producing report after report. Since however this work could not be completed (although there exist some timid attempts today), the serious artist will have to look himself at the work of his predecessors to see what he finds useful, or what has proven its usefulness over the years. This research may lead the serious artist to the very beginning of speech: with his first cry, the first man produced a sound which balanced the emotional content of his sound perfectly with the vocal quality of his sound, both of which, however, were fairly rough and simple. Since then, an ever increasing desire for more subtle statements has resulted in an ever increasing complexity of utterances: You begin with the yeowl and the bark, and you develop into the dance and into music, and into music with words, and finally into words with a vague adumbration of music, words suggestive of music, words measures, or words in a rhythm that preserves some accurate trait of the emotive expression, or of the sheer character of the fostering or parental emotion. ( ibid., p. 51) Since the primeval scream, the complexity of vocal expression has developed such that the variety of detail and range of expression have expanded immensely. This has positive sides - a wider range of detailed expresseion - but it also has become easier to make mistakes. One such mistake is to take three pages to say nothing; it is, in a sense, the cry wolf of the stone age transposed to modernist modernity. The most efficient way of saying nothing is to keep quiet. The serious artist will have to study and make as best and as appropriate a use as he can of the possibilities of his medium. One of the resulting forms of the ongoing development from the bark to the variety of forms that the serious artist can present his observations in is, as noted before, the Henry James novel. Another such form, is the poem. The serious artist must not mistake these two forms as completely different categories of science. The poet can learn from prose as well as from poetry something about effective ways of expression, or the presentation of images, as well as images themselves. A fluid line runs from one extreme to the other, but the fundamental principles are the same; each has to use words, and each wants to say something clearly and effectively in its own category. If there is a difference, "if the two arts must be divided" then Pound suggests the following distinction: In the verse something has come upon the intelligence. In the prose the intelligence has found a subject for its observations. The poetic fact pre-exists. It is precisely because of this fustian that the Parnassiads and epics of the eighteenth century and most of the present-day words of most of our contemporary versifiers are pests and abominations. ( ibid ., pp. 53-4) In Pound's opinion, prose is the medium for the intellect who wishes to concern himself with something intellectually, and premeditatedly. This is "the conscientious formulation" of a fact or an observation. The poetic fact, on the other hand, creeps up on the artist from behind, as it were, a side effect of life - something which dawns upon the poet, and 'will out' (54). To facilitate this 'willing out' and make it more effective, there is the "labour foregoing" - the work of the poet who has been caught by a stealthy poetic fact and now seeks to find the most efficient way of expressing that poetic fact, which will in most cases be in poetry, though not in all. These distinctions, finally, lead to two important categories of serious artists: (ibid. , p. 53): The specialist , who concerns himself with "the patient testing of the media, the patient experiment which shall avail perhaps the artist himself, but is as likely to avail some successor." The dilettante , who wishes as much to preserve items from the past as present something completely new to his audience. He "has no axe to grind for himself. If he be artist as well, he will be none the less eager to preserve the best precedent work. He will drag out 'sources' that prove him less original than his public would have him." These are not mutually exclusive professions. On the contrary, under ideal circumstances a serious artist should try to be both. Only thus can he set out to create 'good', and eventually perhaps even 'great' art. III The Four Forms When this rhythm, or when the vowel and consonantial melody or sequence seems truly to bear the trace of emotion which the poem (for we have now come to the poem) is intended to communicate, we say that this part of the work is good. And 'this part of the work' is by now 'technique'. That 'dry, dull, pedantic' technique, that all bad artists rail against. It is only a part of technique, it is rhythm, cadence, and the arrangements of sounds. In this section, we will go into some of the details of this 'dry, dull, pedantic' technique. From the first scream onwards, four important categories of use have developed. Although they are valid for other 'forms' in which language manifests itself, we will look at them from the perspective of poetry. After all this what both Pound and Eliot themselves were most interested in. The following main division into four forms in which the poetic aspect can express itself are taken from a later work than "The Serious Artist" (1913). As however Eliot places them before that section and as all Pound's concepts fit in neatly into that greater scheme, and as it is elsewhere implied that a number of these comments have been made before scattered in Pound's many other articles and pieces, I have decided to use that division here. Many of the principles gathered here derive from Pound's "don'ts for imagists", rules which he start setting down in the summer of 1912 together with Richard Aldington and "H.D." There must, however, be no mistaking that the literary movements, when Pound was part of them, were mostly Pound's own poetic ideals and most of these lasted much longer than the trendy names of Imagism and Vorticism that lent expression to them. To the four forms, then: Melopoeia - "wherein the words are charged, over and above their plain meaning, with some musical property, which directs the bearing or trend of that meaning." This is what Pound referred to earlier as "rhythm, cadence, and the arrangements of sounds." It is by far the most technical aspect of poetry - what levers, switches and pullies are to the fabrication of machinery, spondee, rhyme and caesura are to the composition of poems. Let the serious poet, however, not look down on this serious aspect of his craft; for, as was Pound's credo , "Technique ... is the test of a man's sincerity." ( ibid. , p. 8.) The main consideration is that of the relationship between meaning and sound - although each can be studied in separation, the ultimate target is a combined effect. A poem is always a tension between constraint and chaos. The spine of a poem is rhythm - a kind of beat from which can then be produced marked variations. Groundrule: "as regarding rhythm: to compose in the sequence of the musical phrase, not in the sequence of a metronome." ( Literary Essays , p. 3) The sequence of the metronome is solitary confinement. The absence of rhythm altogether is anarchy. Inbetween there is the musical phrase. That is, of course, not to say that the poem cannot be written against a metronome, but the metronome can only serve as a rough pencil sketch of the outline for the musical phrase. Like the musician, the poet will consequently have to produce variations that vary the tempo, raise or lower the volume, change the key and other such effects. Here is a list of elements that the serious poet should consider, regrouped from the "don'ts for imagists": Let the neophyte know assonance and alliteration, rhyme immediate and delayed, simple and polyphonic, as a musician would expect to know harmony and counterpoint and all the minutiae of his craft. Don't chop your stuff into separate iambs. Dont' make each line stop dead at the end, and then begin every next line with a heave. Let the beginning of the next line catch the rise of the rhythm wave, unless you want a definite longish pause. Naturally, your rhythmic structure should not destroy the shape of your words, or their natural sound, or their meaning. It is improbable that, at the start, you will be able to get a rhythm structure strong enough to affect them very much though you may fall a victim to all sorts of false stopping due to line ends and caesurae. Let the candidate fill his mind with the finest cadences he can discover, preferably in a foreign language … Let him dissect the lyrics of Goethe coldly into their component sound values, syllables long and short, stressed and unstressed, into vowels and consonants. There is, however, in the best of verse a sort of residue of sound which remains in the ear of the hearer and acts more or less as an organ base. A rhyme must have in it some slight element of surprise if it is to give pleasure; it need not be bizarre or curious, but it must be well used if used at all. In short, behave as a musician, a good musician, when dealing with that phase of your art which has exact parallels in music. The same laws govern, and you are bound by no others. An important aspect in producing such effects is the concept of quantity, which Pound also uses to underline the correspondences between the art of poetry and the art of music. Quantity is the idea that syllables have different lenghts and that these differences can be employed to devise a sort of rhythmical patterning. The concept derives from classical study, but for Pound it meant much more and his observations more than verge on the phonological. The concept of quantity is closely linked with that of vers libre and the discussion that went on about the format in Pound's days, when the form was young and considered unpoetical. As to quantity, it is foolish to suppose that we are incapable of distinguishing a long vowel from a short one, or that we are mentally debarred from ascertaining how many consonants intervene between one vowel and the next. Few phonologists will disagree with Pound today as extensive research in their field has demonstrated the capability of speakers to distinguish between such aspects as duration, voicing, friction, nasalisation, as well as the combined effect of all these together with the place of articulation and the position of the tongue. A simple three-word example derived from the book that taught me the inns and outs of English pronunciation ( Pronunciation for Student Teachers , C. Gussenhoven & A. Broeders. 1981):: cord - cod - cot The three words are of decreasing duration, the allophone of the first word having approximately four times the duration of the last word. This concept can in turn be used to produce a rhythm, or to modify it. The most famous and most quoted example is Pound's "The Return", which Eliot himself quotes as an example of verse that is "really quantitative" (Ezra Pound: His Metrics and his Poems, TCTC) See, they return; ah, see the tentative Movements, and the slow feet The trouble in the pace and the uncertain Wavering! We can try to make a distinction between spondee, dactyls, iambs and trochai, but the main effect is clear without them. There are forced pauses in odd places and the natural stresses of words are placed in awkward positions. The long syllables in the spondee "slow feet" is set off against a succession of four short and unstressed syllables. This effect is also used in the third line, where three short and unstressed syllables follow each other, thus preventing any iambic or trochaic rhythm to establish itself. It is only truly quantitative in that its rhythm is meant to be disruptive so that the rhythm is eventually 'drowned out'. In most other cases rhythm and quantity work together, much like the drummer and the flutist. It is hard to believe that a more appropriate poem can ever be found than "The Return". Consider the following lines (cf. Hugh Kenner, The Pound Era , p. 91): Gods of the winged shoe! With them the silver hounds, sniffing the trace of air! There lines are highly rhythmical. Pound's poem is prophetic - it signals the return of poets who know where to place their feet. The "Wing'd-with-Awe" are the old, muddling poets who had lost the scent of progress, deaf to the sound of their words and insensible or unaware of the rhythm and cadence that a good poem should have. What weaklings these young, timorous vers librists are when compared to the souls of blood, the courageous and bold ancient Greek poets. Pound, however, has picked up the scent again and is not about to let go. In his criticism of modern poetry, Pound would frequently complain of the modern lack of discipline, of technical skill, as for instance below, where he talks about modern practitioners of vers libre : The actual language and phrasing is often as bad as that of our elders without even the excuse that the words are shovelled in to fill the metric pattern or to complete the noise of a rhyme-sound. Whether or not the phrases followed by the followers are musical must be left to the reader's decision. At times I can find a marked metre in 'vers libres', as stale and hackneyed as any pseudo-Swinburnian, at times the writers seem to follow no musical structure whatever. ( Literary Essays , p. 3.) Much of Pound's technical wisdom is reflected in his poetry, whence Eliot says that his prose and poetry should not be read without each other, as they are to a large extent complementary. Another example demonstrates Pound's deep phonological insight: (cf. Hugh Kenner, The Pound Era , p. 91): In nature are signatures needing no verbal tradition, oak leaf never plane leaf. (87/573:609) In the last line, all syllables are "stopped off" by consonants which produces a very disruptive effect. The common phonological effect called linking is thus prevented, and the effect is further enhanced by the unvoiced alveolar, fricative, and plosive which break up the voicing in the first three boundaries. It is a good example of a rhythm forcing itself on the reader, since by the time the last word-boundary is reached, most readers will pronounce the two words separately, disjuncted, even though in normal reading the two words would be linked by voicing which runs from the 'l' in plane up to the 'f' in leaf. In combination with short syllables the effect would be equivalent to that of staccato in music. We are reminded once more, that the serious artist should "In short, behave as a musician, a good musician, when dealing with that phase of your art which has exact parallels in music. The same laws govern, and you are bound by no others." And so the artist is to look for inspiration in both music and this aspect of his work in other poetry, not only in his own language, but in that of other languages too. Or, if he has not the faculty, at the very least he can pay attention to those of his colleagues who do and learn the material second hand from them instead. For that, a serious artist could turn to critics like Pound himself, who made intensive study of Provencal, of Chinese, Japanese, of Latin, Greek, Italian and French poetry. Bearing in mind, that: The melopoeia can be appreciated by a foreigner with a sensitive ear, even though he be ignorant of the language in which the poem is written. It is practically impossible to transfer or translate it from one language to another, save perhaps by divine accident, and for half a line at a time. (p.25) This is because different languages have different sound associations, natural rhythms, stresses and pronunciation features. However, the ultimate goal in this aspect of poetry is producing a significant relationship between sound and sense, and as this effect occurs in other languages too, it can be studied with benefit to the serious artist, who will gain a broader and deeper insight into the fundamental properties and possibilities of language in general. These will help him in mastering these possibilities of his own language. Finally, it can be noted that there are basically three kinds of melopoeia: that made to be sung on a tune that made to be intoned or sung to a sort of chant that made to be spoken Although the third is by now far the most common form, it is nevertheless important to be aware of the three different kinds, because "the art of conjoining words in each of these kinds is different, and cannot be clearly understood until the reader knows that there are three different objectives." The main difference between the three different forms is the prominence of the rhythm. In the third, the natural rhythm of spoken language itself will most prominently determine the musical property of the word and hence "the art of conjoining" or phonetic qualities, syllable length and so forth, much like in the modern poem; whereas in the first these features will largely be determined by an external musical pattern which is imposed on the text, and will call for syllables to be drawn out and modulated. This finds its equivalent in the modern pop-song, opera and musical. The second comes inbetween, where the interplay between rhythm and language is less exact, more equal. Usually a drum beat will determine the rhythm. Most of the standard features of the spoken text remain exact, but some may be stressed and reinforced to accompany and stress the rhythm, and alterations in the intonation patterns will occur. A modern equivalent will be found in rap, older forms include religious chants such as found in Africa. One important consequences of this insight has been mentioned before: When you have words of a lament set to the rhythm and tempo of There'll be a Hot Time in the Old Town to-night you have either an intentional burlesque or you have rotten art. (51) As you see, even rhyme patterns have their histories and associations, and these should not be left unconsidered. For more, see logopoeia , below. Phanopoeia - "which is a casting of images upon the visual imagination" If melopoeia concerns itself with the ear, phanopoeia concerns itself with the eye. Note that this does not include the layout and form of the poem, which is part of architectonics , or the form of the whole. Phanopoeia covers anything from simple and straightforward description to complex symbols and use of metaphor, although in poetry it will specifically concern itself with the latter; for the most simple and straightforward manner of describing something is prose. Groundrule: "To use absolutely no word that does not contribute to the presentation" ( Literary Essays , p. 3) The subject of phanopoeia is too wide and too diverse to treat anywhere near exhaustive. Therefore, Pound concentrates on formulating a few of the most important issues involved. These primaly concern the problem of presentation: When Shakespeare talks of the 'Dawn in russet mantle clad' he presents something which the painter does not present. There is in this line of his nothing that one can call description; he presents. ( ibid. , p. 6) In other words, Shakespeare's dawn is not described, she walks onto the stage. This is closely linked to the Aristotalian method of metaphor, in the form which Pound likes best: Aristotle will tell you that 'the apt use of metaphor, being as it is, the swift perception of relations, is the true hall-mark of genius.' That abundance, that readiness of the figure is indeed one of the surest proofs that the mind is up borne on the emotional surge. By apt use, I should say it were well to understand, a swiftness, almost a violence, and certainly a vividness. This does not mean elaboration and complication.( ibid ., 52) Precision is what counts. Great art is a combination of saying something new or saying something in a new way, and saying something "in such a way that one cannot re-say it more effectively." The trick is to use exactly as much words as you need and not one word more. Although it must be remembered that sometimes a certain amount of background noise can help to offset the primary image, in which case however these words are also necessary as precisely that - background material. Regardless of which method of presentation is being used, two distinctions of method can be made. Or, to put it differently, the presentation can have two different attributes. These are, for a lack of better terms, hardness and softness . The qualities are somewhat hard to describe, but Pound gives a few examples and as he shows a clear prediliction for hard verse, we can derive much of the rest: hardness is a certain kind of minimalism in reproducing or representing the poetic subject. In the writers of the duo-cento and early tre-cento we find a precise psychology, embedded in a now almost unintelligible jargon, but there nevertheless. If we cannot get back to these things; if the serious artist cannot attain this precision in verse, then he must either take to prose or give up his claim to being a serious artist. (53) "Use no superfluous word and no adjective which does not reveal something. (7) One moves the reader only by clarity. In depicting the motions of the 'human heart' the durability of the writing depends on the exactitude. (22) litterati - when their very medium, the very essence of their work, the application of word to things goes rotten, i.e. becomes slushy and inexact, or excessive or bloated, the whole machinery of social and of individual thought and order goes to rot. This is a lesson of history, and a lesson not yet half learned. (21) As the most efficient way to say nothing is to keep quiet, and as technique consists precisely in doing the thing that one sets out to do, in the most efficient manner, no man who takes three pages to say nothing can expect to be seriously considered as a technician. To take three pages to say nothing is not style, in the serious sense of that word. (54) Rimbaud brought back to phanopoeia its clarity and directness (35) Learn to charge words from Flaubert (idem) "Consider the definiteness of Dante's presentation as compared with Milton's. (9) In the art of Daniel and Cavalcanti, I have seen that precision which I miss in the Victorians, that explicit rendering, be it of external nature, or of emotion. Their testimony is of the eyewitness, their symptoms are first-hand. (10) It is precisely because of this fustian that the Parnassiads and epics of the eighteenth century and most of the present-day works of most of our contemporary versifiers are pests and abominations. (53) As for the nineteenth century, with all respect to its achievements, I think we shall look back upon it as a rather blurry, messy sort of period, a rather sentimentalistic, mannerish sort of period. (10) And undoubtedly, the list can be made much longer. Perhaps the strongest evidence of Pound's insistence on this matter is that the first of the three groundrules he established for imagism together with "H.D." and Richard Aldington, is virtually identical to the second: Groundrule: "Direct treatment of the 'thing' whether subjective or objective." ( Literary Essays , p. 3) As you saw in the list, again Pound invites us to consider what men in other languages have done before us, for Phanopoeia can ... be translated almost, or wholly, intact. When it is good enough, it is practically impossible for the translator to destroy it save by very crass bungling, and the neglect of perfectly well-known and formulative rules. Logopoeia - 'the dance of the intellect among words', that is to say, it employs words not only for their direct meaning, but it takes count in a special way of habits of usage, of the context we expect to find the word, its usual concomitants, of its known acceptances, and of ironical play. It holds the aesthetic content which is peculiarly the domain of verbal manifestation, and cannot possibly be contained in plastics or music. It is the latest come, and perhaps most tricky and undependable mode." This is the mode which reached a definite climax during modernism. Pound credits Laforgue with the (re)discovery of this tricky aspect of poetry. Its popularity and interest was further boosted by works such as the production of great dictionaries and etymological works, Jung's archetypes, Freud's dream interpretations and Frazer's cultural analysis, most notably The Golden Bough . All this was combined with an increasing awareness of the presence of the past in the present strengthened by the awakening belief that the principles of Darwinian evolution existed in areas besides biology. To give an idea of the complexity of this concept in the field of prosody, we can say that logopoeia covers a vast range of linguistic categories, most notably connotation and collocation, among which also punning, all the way through etymology to cultural history, all of which can be used to understand and control the use of words, as well as make them more effective in doing so. It is not for nothing that Pound quotes Frazer's Golden Bough as a book which displays the "implements by which one can pour." ( ibid. , p. 32) Detailed cultural history of ancient mythologies, cult, ritual and their interrelations with Christianity helped to expose the enormous history that hid behind a great number of contemporary cultural phenomenon, and a skilled poet will be able to resuscitate all this with only a few words, thus achieving a much more intense of expression. The trickyness of this mode, however, does not only lie in its complexity but perhaps even more its undependablity. As historical research uncovers the different contexts in which words have been used, we also become aware of word-use being subject to considerable change. The shifty nature of the word and symbol causes different readers to read or see different things; not everyone possesses equal amounts of background knowledge. The partial solution to this aspect of the problem of logopoeia is closely linked to the work of the dilettante and the serious artist. First, let us remember the general idea that lies behind the work of both, the study of his predecessors. It is a foolish thing for a man to begin his work on a too narrow foundation, it is a disgraceful thing for a man's work not to show steady growth and increased fineness from first to last. (9) As for 'adaptations'; one finds that all the old masters of painting recommend to their pupils that they begin by copying masterwork and proceed to their own composition.(9) Now, consider the following point: "Be influenced by as many great artists as you can, but have the decency either to acknowledge the debt outright or try to conceal it. Besides showing you are a decent as well as a serious artist and work in accordance with (current) practice in most scientific research and publication, there is obviously another advantage to acknowledging your debt: by somehow indicating your source-material, by somehow identifying the works of your predecessors that have helped you create your own work, the reader will be in the possession of a key to unlock the precedent work on which the newer work builds. Through being able to check the sourcetext of the work that the dilettante builds upon, the reader who lacks suficient background knowledge (or possesses too much, for that matter) will be able to figure out what the artist was up to, and also perhaps determine whether he has succeeded or failed. Again, a final word of advice from Pound on the study of other languages: "Logopoeia does not translate; although the attitude of mind it expresses may pass through a paraphrase. Or one might say, you can not translate it ‘locally’, but having determined the original author’s state of mind, you may or may not be able to find a derivative or an equivalent" (25). Architectonics - "the form of the whole." "Any work of art is a compound of freedom and order. It is perfectly obvious that art hangs between chaos on the one side and mechanics on the other. A pedantic insistence upon detail tends to drive out 'major form'. A firm hold on major form makes for a freedom of detail. In painting men intent on minutiae gradually lost the sense of form and form-combination. The form of the whole is not just the shape of the poem - the sonnet, or the lyric, or the ballad, the epic poem, the Sapphic ode, the alexandrine, the heroic couplet, and so on. Rather, it is a kind of unity that works on all levels mentioned so far. Consider a castle with magnificent towers, which, however, are far too large in comparison with the puny walls to which they form the corner. Consider a musical piece that consists of a number of near-perfect technical and compositional gems, without, however, any larger division in movements or any dramatic development. Consider a play full of brilliant jokes but no plot. Or, consider a painting that whether you look at it from afar or with a magnifying glass, does not disclose anything other than beautiful little round dots with a chaotic pattern. None of these examples are bad art in themselves; their success depends on whether the artist achieves the effect he set out to create. What is important, is the consideration of form in creating the work of art. If, for instance, a serious artist wished to create a sonnet, then he should have an idea to express which can be fitted precisely into a sonnet. It is no use fitting the idea in the couplet and then fill up the octet and sextet with meaningless detail that does nothing to enhance the effect of the couplet. Or, to turn the matter around, when an idea taps you on the shoulder, turn around and look at it carefully to see what kind of form it would wear most gracefully, that would not only fit the idea tightly, but could even contribute to the expression, the intensity of that idea. Conclusion What I've been able to do here is little more than make a coherent sketch of Pound's theoretical heritage. Perhaps such coherence was not always apparent to readers, but I believe that those who were familiar enough with his work, like Eliot, were able to see enough of a unity to be profoundly influenced. There were undoubtedly other poets, and perhaps this overview will help to identify them and determine how far the influence went; where the disagreements were and so forth. One poet who was at least partially under the influence of Pound was Marianne Moore, evidence of which is given in The Pound Era . Eliot, however, was perhaps closest to Pound and perhaps also most strongly influenced. There are numerous effects in Eliot's poetry, such as the working of shards of popular songs, extensive allusions to and borrowings from influences, use of various rhythmical formats and metrical effects, foreign influences, and much more. We see Eliot's attack on vers libre, so much like Pound's attack on vers libre, with the same arguments. We read essays like The Music of Poetry, which, 30 years after Pound started formulating and stressing the important and instructive similarities between the two media, are little more than a rehashing of what Pound said, in a milder form. An essay like Tradition and the Individual Talent is, in the light of this reformulation, little more than a somewhat romantic rephrasing of Pound's idea that an artist should know the work of his predecessors, on a slightly larger scale. Almost conform to Pound's criterium of the dilettante , Eliot acknowledges his debt in the last paragraph of part I of the essay: There remains to define this process of depersonalization and its relation to the sense of tradition. It is in this depersonalization that art may be said to approach the condition of science. I shall, therefore, invite you to consider, as a suggestive analogy, the action which takes place when a bit of finely filiated platinum is introduced into a chamber containing oxygen and sulphur dioxide. Like Pound, Eliot makes an analogy between the work of art and of science. He stresses the importance of the past in relation to the present. His focus on 'what is already living' is congruous with Pound's idea that poetry should be written in the medium of the present, unless there is a very good reason to revive a medium from the past (for instance the stricture of the quatrain form as a counterforce to excesses of the diluters of bad vers libre ). Certainly, there are influences from Bradley's concept of history, and perhaps even from Joyce, who occasionally mentions the concept of personalisation for instance in Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man , not to mention Wordsworth's introduction to the Lyrical Ballads . But the process of thinking about the art of poetry in this manner is unmistakably in the style of Pound's serious artist. Perhaps the strongest evidence of Pound's influence in this respect is Eliot's statement that his critical writing was more or less a side-effect of his poetry-writing. It shows that Eliot was thinking about his poetry in a deliberate, conscious, and near scientific manner much in agreement with Pound's definition of the Serious Artist. There is much more, but that lies beyond the scope of this essay, which intends to provide a point of reference and a springboard for further research. An attempt to pave the way for other serious artists - or critics for that matter.

title: Bleistein and Shylock
text: Bleistein and Shylock in "Burbank with a Baedeker: Bleistein with a Cigar" Venice by the late 16th century had become what New York is in literature today: a symbolic city of decaying and corrupt power. Today Eliot might put it in line with the rest of his falling towers, taking the place of the Unreal City. But in his time Eliot put Chicago there, expecting that city to be first, and besides that knowing the city much better. Making a line in the poem Venice-Vienna-Chicago has a number of obvious reasons; each of these cities was in its time a, or even the, major trading city and partly as a consequence, partly as a condition, these cities consisted of many different ethnologies. In Eliot’s continuous line of past-present-future lies a warning to Chicago; Venice fell apart from racial dispute, Vienna fell or was falling apart for this same reason during the period of Eliot’s conception of this poem, and Chicago threatened to fall apart in the near future. That is not to say that racial disputes were the only reasons for these cities’ failings; but in his compact poem, Eliot managed to make several points by symbolic representation, and this is but one of them. The particular symbol he chose for representing race is the same which Joyce had chosen in Ulysses. Presumably for that reason the first line of the poem, Burbank crossed a little bridge, is a reference to Joyce’s Portrait of the Artist; where it reads that Stephen symbolically decides to leave the clergy because he believes he will be able to learn more from dwelling among sin. The second line, descending at a small hotel, is then presumably a reference to Freud’s interpretation of dreams, where the phrase figures prominently in connection to a dream where the subject also dwell among sin – in this particular case prostitution. Henry Adams, referred to in the phrase Time’s Ruins, wrote out the idea of going to Europe to dwell among sin and to learn from that in his Education. In one of Henry James’ essays on Venice, after dwelling romantically on the city’s picturesque atmosphere, momentarily awakens from the dream to harsh reality. Where does this happen? In the tourist industry, owned by the Jews. He finds it ugly, but true, as the truth is often ugly just as beauty is often a lie. It was Joyce’s conception in Portrait of the Artist, developed in Ulysses, to describe something ugly such that it became beautiful. Leopold Bloom was his ultimate exercise – an apparently ugly, ironical anti-hero taking the place of the heroic Ulysses in a 48 hour Odyssey. Combine the two images, and you have Bleistein in Eliot’s poem. The complexity of Eliot’s poem derives from its seemingly endless references to literary sources and the intricacy and compactness with which they have been employed. We’ll look at one important reference in detail, ‘on the Rialto once’. … On the Rialto once. The rats are underneath the piles. The Jew is underneath the lot. Money in furs. … On the Rialto once immediately calls up Shylock; by this sixth stanza Eliot’s method of evoking Venice by employing literary and symbolic references to that city and works related to that city should have become clear to an educated reader. There is no single play nearly as well known as The Merchant of Venice in which the phrase occurs quite nearly so often (eleven times), and then when we further note that Shylock is probably the most famous of (solely) literary Jews there can be little doubt that this text is being referred to. On a slightly closer inspection we can even identify a particular speech from Shakespeare’s play: Signoir Antonio, many a times and oft In the Rialto you have rated me About my moneys and my usances: Still I have borne it with a patient shrug, (For suff’rance is the badge of all our tribe) You call me misbeliever, cut-throat dog, And spet upon my Jewish gaberdine, And all for use of that which is mine own. Several of these accusations return in Eliot’s poem – the patient shrug is a representation of the Jew’s posture which has become a point of mockery even in Eliot’s own time, as several cartoon representations of Jews in that time attest to. Eliot has taken this over in his description of the saggy bending of the knees and elbows, and the palms turned out, combining it with the image of Christ on the cross, representations of which will nine out of ten times fit the description. One particular term of abuse in here is the ‘cut-throat dog’ which returns in the play a few times; continuously the Jew is being compared to animals, and among these the dog (and the wolf) figure most prominently. This has a reason, certainly in Shakespeare’s play. We remember that Antonio is despondent at the beginning of the play, having a sense of evil foreboding. With one of the most suspect lines Shakespeare ever uses – ‘let me play the fool’ (after Antonio had said that the world’s a stage on which each must play his part and himself a sad one) – Gratiano, a friend to Antonio, comments upon the foolishness of those who try to be what they are not; try to evoke wisdom by saying nothing or to show on the outside what is not on the inside: There are a sort of men whose visages Do cream and mantle like a standing pond, And do a wilful stillness entertain, With purpose to be dress’d in an opinion Of wisdom, gravity, profound conceit, As who should say, "I am Sir Oracle, And when I ope my lips, let no dog bark." As usual, the fool has all the wisdom and his words are the only truly oracular ones in the play. "I am Sir Oracle, / And when I ope my lips, let no dog bark" refers to the proverbial expression that those at whom the dog barks are in disgrace. When Shylock barks at Antonio, the close reader will know what time it is. Admittedly, it’s a very small detail to catch. But it’s not the only one – following directly on the speech above, read the following: O my Antonio, I do know of these That therefore only are reputed wise For saying nothing; when I am very sure If they should speak, would almost damn those ears Which (hearing them) would call their brothers fools This last line is a reference to Matthew v.22, which reads as follows: "But I say unto you, That whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment: and whosoever shall say to his brother, Raca, shall be in danger of the council: but whosoever shall say, Thou fool, shall be in danger of hell fire." It is in Matthew 22.31-45 that a lawyer tests Jesus’ knowledge of the scriptures by asking him what the most important commandment in the Bible is: "Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind. This is the first and great commandment. And the second [is] like unto it, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself." Several of the warnings implicit in Gratiano’s speech hold true for Antonio, who abuses Shylock for no reason, wherefore he is in danger of judgment. This Antonio receives, and by Shylock’s bond, who as he says himself, assumes the vengeance of the part of misinterpreted Christian law – if each character in the play plays a part, Gratiano the fool, Antonio a sad one, then Shylock plays justice. Antonio learns his lesson from him during the course of the play in the terms that a merchant can understand: For the commodity that strangers have With us in Venice, if it be denied, Will much impeach the justice of the state, Since that the trade and profit of the city Consisteth of all nations. It is the truth that Blake’s "The Clod and the Pebble" so eloquently expresses, and it is probably no coincidence that Burbank takes the place of innocence and Bleistein that of experience, at least at the beginning of the poem – Burbank is "tra-la-la-la-lair", Bleistein "nil nisi divinum stabile est; caetera fumus". But, like Antonio, Burbank (through playing the parts of several literary Antonios throughout the poem) comes to learn his lesson, expressed in the final stanza of the poem. The bottom line is that Eliot has recognised the Jew as a symbol for the danger of not loving your neighbour, on both a moral and an economical scale. The trade and profit of the city, whether it be London, Venice, Vienna, or Chicago, consisteth of all Nations and the justice of any state is much ‘impeached’ (how true this word sounds today) by denying this justice and by not loving thy neighbour like yourself. This idea, together with that other great point expressed by Gratiano and echoed in the casket episode and in nearly all of Shakespeare’s works that involve prejudice in one form or another, which is that appearances are deceptive, including that of Venice and her personification in the poem, Princess Volupine, returns in several other places in the poem and in the other texts referred to by the poem. But this single instance is a crucial turning-point in the poem deserving of much more comment than ‘oh look – how racist of Eliot’. The words are Bleistein’s, barking like Shylock at a person in disgrace, but more with the silent style of the man in black in Browning’s poem. The price for not heeding this call was WWI, the malice of the post-WWI treaties, and the world-wide recession following. Perhaps he also foresaw that the mistake would be made again, in part through his professional confrontation with the Versailles treaty which carries all of the faults of revenge (in Shakespeare’s play also represented by Shylock, but he for the moment assumes the role of the Christian avenger, as he himself is ever pursued for something that happened in generations before him) and none of the love (so typical of Shakespeare, and in The Merchant represented by Portia). As Eliot would say himself much later: It is a symptom of the deepest dilemma, chaos and of the malfunctioning of the economy and in the religious pretensions of that nation; and is used to the point of exploitation by rulers as a radical remedy which, in the end, only aggravates the disease of which it is a symptom. These remarks provide a more reasonable ground for the conviction which I have long held, that any country which denies the rights of its own citizens or makes pariahs of any body of its own nationals - and most especially the Jews - will sooner or later have to pay the full price for so doing; and even the 'uninvolved' people whom it governs will have to expiate the crime of having allowed such a government to lead them.' sources

title: The Sisters, Tennyson
text: The Sisters Alfred Tennyson WE were two daughters of one race: She was the fairest in the face The wind is blowing in turret and tree. They were together, and she fell; Therefore revenge became me well. O the Earl was fair to see! She died she went to burning flame: She mix'd her ancient blood with shame. The wind is howling in turret and tree. Whole weeks and months, and early and late, To win his love I lay in wait: O the Earl was fair to see! I made a feast; I bad him come; I won his love, I brought him home. The wind is roaring in turret and tree. And after supper, on a bed, Upon my lap he laid his head: O the Earl was fair to see! I kiss'd his eyelids into rest His ruddy cheek upon my breast. The wind is raging in turret and tree, I hated him with the hate of hell, But I loved his beauty passing well. O the Earl was fair to see! I rose up in the silent night I made my dagger sharp and bright. The wind is raving in turret and tree. As half-asleep his breath he drew, Three times I stabb'd him thro' and thro'. O the Earl was fair to see! I curl'd and comb'd his comely head, He look'd so grand when he was dead. The wind is blowing in turret and tree. I wrapt his body in the sheet, And laid him at his mother's feet. O the Earl was fair to see!

title: Tennyson Note
text: They were... From Alfred Tennyson's "The Sisters". Note that Tennyson's poem reads 'she' instead of 'he.' reference topics Tennyson's The Sisters "The Sisters" and "Burbank" Structural analysis back to "Burbank"

title: The Story of St.
text: The Story of St. Sebastian in "Burbank with a Baedeker: Bleistein with a Cigar" and "The Love Song of St. Sebastian" Sebastian was a Roman captain who secretly adhered the Christian faith. When this was discovered by the emperor, Diocletian, he ordered Sebastian to be assassinated by mercenary archers. Sebastian is said to have survived the arrows, however, and was restored to health by a Christian widow, Irene. He returned to accuse the emperor of cruelty, who then had him clubbed to death and dumped in a sewer. Through this story, St. Sebastian temporarily obtained the status of the first Christian martyr. Salient detail is that in a television series ( Millenium ) there is an episode in which the mummified hand of St Sebastian appears as a talisman with magic powers - however possesses the hand will be the dominant political leader.

title: Baedeker
text: Andrea Mantegna's St Sebastian in "Burbank with a Baedeker: Bleistein with a Cigar" and "The Love Song of St. Sebastian" Andrea Mantegna's "St Sebastian" (Ca d'Oro, Venice). introduction The section Eliot quotes in "Burbank," nil nisi divinum stabile est; caetera fumus , is an inscription on a banner that is attached to a small candle in the right-hand corner of the painting. The burning candle symbolises the soul and via Bleistein's cigar and the line "the smoky candle end of time this symbol in particular is attached to the poem and to Bleistein. linguistical analysis The latin phrase translates "nothing if not the divine is certain; all else smoke." On the painting, the first word is actually nihil, of which nil is a common contraction and presumably Eliot contracted it in his mind. Caetera fumus is a common Latin form to express a simile, so that we can in fact read "all else is like smoke." textual/poetical analysis The grave latin inscription is visually strenghtened by the candle, which slowly burns up and turns to smoke, a metaphor for life and history. This image returns in Eliot's poem later on, when "The smokey candle end of time" declines. Together with the martyred St. Sebastian on the painting, it makes for a very stark contrast with the quotation from Gautier. As noted elsewhere, there is a strong parallellism running through this poem, in the title, in the epigraph, and in the structure of the poem itself. There is the textual suggestion of the number seven in both number of words in the Latin phrase and the seven constituents of the phrase "tra-la-la-la-la-la-laire", which is not exactly a copy of Gautier's line (see previous note). The candle in this picture invites to draw another parallel by tying in to the declining "smoky candle end of time" in stanza five; the suggestion is that there are similarities between the martyred figure in the painting, St Sebastian, and Bleistein. contextual analysis The origin for the phrase is unknown, although the concept itself presumably originates with Plato's concept of the divine, which was rooted in the idea-world. Presumably this concept has survived somehow into the 1500s through the Neo-Platinists, or St Augustine, or a rediscovery of any of these sources during the Italian renaissance. Mantegna has made three paintings of the St Sebastian, one of which hangs in Vienna, one in Paris, and one (this one) in Venice. The theme for all of these paintings was the then prevalent preoccupation with the fall of Rome. Especially artists found the inventions of their predecessors of over 15 centuries ago very impressive, and the resultant mood was one of contemplation over, exactly, the concepts of decline and death. The artistic (and public) subconsciousness was further oppressed by the then rampant plague, which caused millions of casualties throughout Europe. This theme is expressed in the paintings in several ways. In the Vienna Sebastian this is most clearly seen in the symbolic union of two different styles of arches to which Sebastian is bound, and in the landscapes behind the arch, one of which is a reconstruction of the hay-days Roman times, the other the same landscape but as it is now. In the Venice painting, the most clear symbol is certainly the burning candle, signifying the passage of time and clarified by the banderole with the inscription. Of course, St. Sebastian himself is such a symbol too, as the Empire which executed him has in Mantegna's days made place for the religion that Sebastian was martyred for. In those days, Sebastian also came to be seen as a protector against the plague. One of the reasons was that arrow-wounds looked a lot like plague-marks. Thus the arrows came to symbolise the onslaught of the disease, which further explains the popularity of him as a subject for painters in those days. (Greenstein) As we can see in stanzas four through six, there are a number of similarities between what happens to Bleistein and St. Sebastian. First of all, the description of Bleistein in the first paragraph resembles that of the martyred St. Sebastian, who also has his palms turned out, his elbows bent, as well as one knee. There is also one staring eye, and although because of the shadow this cannot be seen clearly, the differing position of the left eyebrow seems to suggest that the other eye is closed (#! further detailed study of this painting or at least a larger reproduction will be necessary to confirm this). Eliot invites us to draw a parallel between St. Sebastian and Bleistein; there are certainly paralels with Bleistein's memory of the insults against Shylock. These insults, if we read their imagery literally, also provide a link; the Jew that Bleistein remembers was perhaps killed and dumped on the street - if we examine instances of combinations of lustreless with staring eyes this often means dead - with garbage and rats on him, which seems not wholly dissimilar from St. Sebastian's fate of ending up in a sewer - there will, at the very least, also be rats there. One historical implication is that the Jew Bleistein assumes the role of martyr similar to the position of the Christians in the Roman empire. Certainly, if we do compare the situation of the Jews in a historical context, a striking parallel exists between the constant and persistent persecutions of the Jews since Christianity rose to power in Europe, and the persecutions of the Christians by the Romans when they were still an oppressed group together with the Hebrew people. Furthermore, arrows are traditionally also symbols of verbal assaults, something which Jews have been historically prone to, not the least during the strong rise of anti-Semitism from the turn of the century up to WWII. There are other possibilities, however, as in Faust: Hovering around this head in circles airy, Look that ye show the noble law of fairy: Appease the furious conflict in his heart! Draw out the burning arrows of remorse, From suffered horrors cleanse his inmost part! We could easily combine this image with Bleistein's remembrance of Shylock's remorse, where it is good to remember that the remorse in Shakespeare's play is in turn caused by Antonio's constant spurning of Shylock. However, the association between the Jews as Martyrs against the Christians as martyrs seems to be the central image, the rest are details. The comparison his painful, and the irony indeed, as Eliot himself said several times in his letters, "intensely serious." Whether it is also far-fetched remains to be seen.
